How do you hope to shape or support the Wagner community?

column2:
TIME MANAGEMENT ESSAY 

MEM challenges its students with rigorous content and, at times, a fast-paced learning environment. Describe a situation where you leveraged time management skills to ensure the success of a project. Please ensure your response is 300 words or less and addresses the following: 

1. What lessons did you learn from the experience? 

2. How can you translate those skills into being a successful MEM student?
column7:
Crux of the Topic:
This prompt evaluates your ability to plan, prioritize, and execute effectively under pressure — a critical skill for success in Northwestern's fast-paced MEM environment. The admissions team wants a real, results-driven story that demonstrates your discipline, adaptability, and growth mindset.
column8:
What’s the most complex project you’ve successfully managed under pressure?

What specific time management tools or habits helped you stay focused?

How did your approach affect your team or final outcome?

What did you learn about planning or adaptability from this experience?

How will you manage multiple demands during MEM’s fast-paced curriculum?

column2:
Your essay should address the following 3 questions. We recommend that your total essay length should be 700 words minimum (up to 1100 words suggested maximum). It is acceptable, but not required, to copy and paste the questions below into your essay. 

 

What are your career goals after completing graduate school? (Recommended response length: 150-300 words) 

 

How have your prior experiences (professional, academic, and/or personal) prepared you for graduate study and informed your career goals?  

 

(Recommended response length: 400-500 words) 

How will attending this degree program at Heinz College help you achieve your career goals? (Recommended response length: 150-300 words
column7:
Crux of the Topic:
Heinz College seeks applicants who are purpose-driven, academically ready, and clearly understand how the program supports their trajectory. They want to see evidence of career vision, preparation, and program fit in a structured, cohesive narrative.
column8:
What specific role or field do you want to pursue after Heinz?

Which past experience most directly shaped your goal or passion?

What skills or insights do you still need to develop?

How does Heinz’s curriculum or ethos stand out to you?

How do you hope to make an impact in your chosen field?

column2: Identify a problem in domestic or international affairs, discuss its complexity, and propose policy recommendations. The memo should demonstrate the ability to provide critical analysis of an issue, to write cogently, and offer reasonable and realistic recommendations.
column7:
Crux of the Topic:
Princeton SPIA is evaluating your analytical sharpness, clarity of writing, and policy fluency. They want to see that you can define a real-world policy problem, assess its multi-dimensional complexity, and offer practical, well-reasoned policy solutions — all in a structured, cogent format.
column8:
What domestic or global issue are you most compelled to improve or reform?

What are the root causes and dimensions of this issue?

What existing policies have failed or fallen short — and why?

Who are the key stakeholders involved in change (and resistance)?

What targeted solutions would you propose, and why are they feasible?

column2: Share with us the most amazing experience you have had far away from your comfort zone? And the most shocking one? And the worst?
column7:
Crux of the Topic:
ESADE wants to evaluate your resilience, adaptability, self-awareness, and emotional intelligence. This prompt is designed to see how you respond to unfamiliar situations — both positive and challenging — and how you reflect on growth through discomfort.
column8:
When did you last feel truly surprised or disoriented in a new setting?

What’s a travel or cultural moment that amazed you?

When did you face a setback or failure far from home or familiarity?

What personal strengths helped you through these moments?

How do you now approach unfamiliar challenges because of these experiences?

column2: We want to get to know you on a more personal and individual basis. To do so, applicants must respond to this prompt: “We all come from somewhere and it shapes us — both in who we become and what we value. Tell us your story and what has shaped you.”
column7:
Crux of the Topic:
This prompt is designed to uncover your core identity, values, and motivations. Princeton wants to understand the formative influences — cultural, familial, geographic, social, or personal — that shape how you see the world and interact with others.
column8:
What part of your background has most shaped your identity?

What’s a story that captures your family or cultural roots?

How did your upbringing influence your values or goals?

What challenges or privileges come from where you come from?

How has your story prepared you to contribute to Princeton’s community?

column2: Describe with examples the most important personal quality that you possess, which significantly enhances your prospects of being successful as a leader?
column7:
Crux of the Topic:
ISB is assessing your self-awareness, leadership maturity, and ability to reflect on what makes you effective in group or organizational contexts. They want to see not just the quality, but how it has been proven through real actions.
column8:
What personal trait do others most consistently recognize or admire in you?

When did this trait help you solve a major challenge or conflict?

How does this trait influence the way you lead teams or communicate?

When did you grow or strengthen this quality through experience?

How will this trait help you contribute at ISB and in your post-MBA career?

column2: Please outline the reasons why you are suited or well-qualified for this course, your motivation for applying and how you will benefit from the course. Please add any other information that you feel will help us to assess your application. The statement should be 500 - 1000 words in length, in English, and be your own words
column7:
Crux of the Topic:
The University of York wants to assess your academic fit, clarity of motivation, and potential for impact through the program. They are looking for evidence that you are prepared, purpose-driven, and understand how the course aligns with your aspirations.
column8:
What experiences have most prepared you for this course?

What is your core motivation for studying this subject now?

Which aspects of the course at York excite you the most?

What career or research goals do you hope to achieve through this degree?

What additional qualities or interests would you want the admissions team to know?

column1: Skill you bring to the table
column2: Please tell us something about yourself, your experiences, or activities that you believe would reflect positively on your ability to succeed at Penn State. This is your opportunity to tell us something about yourself that is not already reflected in your application or academic records. We suggest a limit of 500 words or fewer.
column7:
Crux of the Topic:
Penn State wants a deeper look at who you are beyond academics and test scores. This essay should highlight personal traits, meaningful experiences, or passions that showcase your potential to thrive in a rigorous, community-driven university environment.
column8:
What’s something about you that your grades or resume don’t show?

What’s an experience or challenge that shaped your growth or outlook?

What personal trait do others rely on you for?

What’s a passion or interest that you’ve pursued outside of school?

How will this story connect to how you’ll succeed and contribute at Penn State?

column2: Describe in detail your programming experience and technical skills (300-350 words)
column7:
Crux of the Topic:
Georgia Tech is assessing your technical foundation, problem-solving ability, and readiness for advanced coursework or research. This essay helps them understand not just what you’ve learned, but how you’ve applied your skills in real-world or academic contexts.
column8:
What programming languages and tools are you most confident in using?

What technical projects or work experiences best showcase your skills?

Have you worked with teams, frameworks, or platforms (e.g., Git, React, AWS)?

What technical challenge taught you the most?

How do you keep your skills current and deepen your coding expertise?

column2:
Why have you selected this course? What are your motivating factors? 

What are your areas of interest within the course? 

What contributions do you feel you can make to the course? 

How do you see the course affecting your career plans? 

What evidence of research or reading in this area can you show?
column7:
Crux of the Topic:
Warwick Business School is assessing your motivation, academic and professional preparedness, clarity of career direction, and your fit with the course community. They want to see genuine enthusiasm, strategic planning, and evidence of your engagement with the subject.
column8:
Why is this course the right fit for your professional path right now?

What specific topics or trends in business most capture your interest?

What skills or experiences will help you contribute uniquely to this cohort?

Where do you see yourself professionally in the next 3–5 years?

What books, articles, or research have shaped your interest in this field?

column2: In 250-500 words please describe your educational goals and those academic and/or professional achievements which have contributed most to your development. Also indicate your expected career track (examples include, but are not limited to, investment advisory, equity or credit analysis, professional accounting, consulting, and research) and describe how the Master of Science in Accounting and Fundamental Analysis will help you attain your career goals
column7:
Crux of the Topic:
Columbia is looking for candidates who have a clear academic and career vision, supported by past achievements, and who can articulate why this specific program is essential to their future success. They want to see focus, depth, and relevance in your goals.
column8:
What role in finance or accounting are you most excited to pursue — and why?

What coursework, job, or project best prepared you for this field?

What have you learned from past financial or investment-related experiences?

Which Columbia resources or strengths align most with your goals?

How do you envision using your Columbia degree in 5–10 years?

column2:
Compare and contrast your experiences working on a high-performing and low-performing team. Please ensure your response is 300 words or less and addresses the following: 

1. Describe a situation where you had to apply critical thinking skills to solve a problem in the workplace? 

2. What contributed to or led to the high/low performance of both teams?  

3. Could you have done anything to shift the low-performing team to a high-performing team?
column7:
Crux of the Topic:
Northwestern wants to assess your teamwork insight, problem-solving ability, and emotional intelligence. They’re evaluating how you adapt to different team dynamics and how you apply critical thinking to solve challenges — especially in contrasting team settings.
column8:
What made your best team click — values, skills, structure?

What caused dysfunction in your low-performing team?

When did you personally step in to solve a team issue?

What lessons did you carry from one experience to the other?

What could you have done to improve the struggling team?

column2:
Optional Essay 

In engineering, diversity of thought and perspectives can lead to innovative solutions and breakthroughs. Reflect on an instance where you collaborated with individuals from diverse backgrounds or worked on a team with varying expertise. How did this diversity enhance the problem-solving process, and what did you learn about the significance of inclusion in engineering projects? How will you actively promote diversity and inclusivity within your future engineering career?
column7:
Crux of the Topic:
This prompt assesses your awareness of diversity’s value, particularly in engineering problem-solving, and your personal commitment to inclusive environments.
column8:
What team experience best showed the value of diverse thinking?

What creative or effective solution came from that diversity?

How did that shift your view on inclusion in STEM?

How do you create space for different perspectives?

What will inclusion look like in your future career?

column2: Describe a unique perspective, skill set, or life experience you have. Articulate how it might benefit your potential classmates, enhance discourse in the classroom, and/or contribute to the NYU Wagner community (250 words)
column7:
Crux of the Topic:
NYU Wagner wants to assess your individuality, voice, and potential to enrich the classroom and school community. This is your chance to present what sets you apart and how your background, experiences, or skills can spark meaningful dialogue, collaboration, or insight.
column8:
What lived experience most shaped your approach to service or leadership?

What uncommon skill or cultural insight do you bring to policy work?

How do you typically help teams see blind spots or new angles?

When did your background offer unique value to a diverse group?

How will this perspective make NYU Wagner stronger?

column2: what makes you a unique candidate for the program in comparison to other applicants? (300-350 words)
column7:
Crux of the Topic:
Georgia Tech is looking for what sets you apart — not generic strengths, but distinctive qualities, experiences, and perspectives that differentiate you from peers. They want evidence of impact, creativity, and readiness to thrive in a rigorous, collaborative environment.
column8:
What’s a quality or experience most peers in your field won’t have?

How have you solved a problem differently than others might have?

What diverse perspective do you bring to collaborative learning?

How do your background and skills align with Georgia Tech’s ethos?

What lasting impact do you want to make on your cohort?

column2: Please tell us something about yourself, your experiences, or activities that you believe would reflect positively on your ability to succeed at Penn State. column7:
Crux of the Topic:
This optional essay is not for repeating accomplishments, but for addressing potential concerns or providing important context that strengthens your application. ESADE’s Admissions Committee uses it to understand nuances beyond your transcript, test scores, or resume.

Do you have any employment gaps or academic dips needing explanation?

Have you retaken (or do you plan to retake) the GMAT or GRE?

column2: A hallmark of the Columbia experience is being able to learn and thrive in an equitable and inclusive community with a wide range of perspectives. Tell us about an aspect of your own perspective, viewpoint, or lived experience that is important to you, and describe how it has shaped the way you would learn from and contribute to Columbia’s diverse and collaborative community.
column7:
Crux of the Topic
Columbia wants to understand:

Who you are beyond your résumé — what life experiences or perspectives define you

Your capacity for empathy, openness, and collaboration

How you engage with different viewpoints and contribute to an inclusive environment

How your background enriches classroom discussions and the broader community

This is not about restating achievements. It’s about identity, growth, and interpersonal value.

How to Approach It
Select one defining perspective or lived experience

It should be authentic, specific, and formative.

Examples: navigating cross-cultural environments, working in underrepresented communities, overcoming systemic barriers, multilingual legal practice, or bridging disciplines.

Illustrate with a brief, vivid example

Use a short anecdote to show how this perspective was developed or tested.

Show its influence on your learning style

How it shapes the way you approach debates, problem-solving, and collaboration.

Link to Columbia

How this perspective will help you both learn from and give to peers in a diverse LLM cohort.
column8:
Which personal experience most shaped your worldview?

What challenges or insights came from navigating that experience?

How has it influenced the way you learn or work with others?

How would you apply this perspective in Columbia’s diverse classrooms?

What unique value will your peers gain from your viewpoint?

column2: What is your earliest memory of an impactful moment?
column7:
Crux of the Topic
Columbia is looking to see:

Emotional depth and self-awareness — how you process formative experiences

Values and motivations rooted in your early life

Storytelling ability — can you convey meaning in a concise, powerful way

How the seeds of your legal or personal ethos were planted

How to Approach It
Choose a single, vivid memory

Ideally from early childhood or adolescence

Could involve fairness, justice, resilience, cross-cultural understanding, or compassion

Recount the moment in sensory detail

Briefly set the scene (who, where, when)

Show what happened and why it mattered

Reflect on its lasting impact

Tie it to your present worldview or professional calling

Avoid generic statements — keep it personal and specific
column8:
What’s your earliest clear memory tied to fairness or injustice?

Who was with you in that moment, and what did they do?

What emotions did you feel then, and how do you interpret them now?

Did this moment influence your later choices or values?

How might this memory connect to your legal aspirations?

column2: Please discuss your past academic and professional experiences and accomplishments that will help you succeed in the Master of Finance program. Include achievements in finance, math, statistics, and computer sciences, as applicable.
column7:
Crux of the Topic
MIT Sloan is assessing:

Quantitative readiness — proven skills in math, statistics, programming, and finance theory

Applied excellence — internships, research, or projects showing you can use theory in practice

Analytical problem-solving ability — especially in complex, data-driven contexts

Track record of achievement in both academic and professional settings

How to Approach It
Lead with strongest academic preparation

Relevant coursework (e.g., calculus, linear algebra, probability, econometrics, machine learning)

Academic honors or research in quantitative fields

Showcase professional accomplishments

Finance-related roles, internships, or projects with measurable impact

Quantitative or computational work (e.g., financial modeling, risk analytics, algorithmic trading)

Demonstrate integration of skills

Instances where finance, math, and tech came together to solve a real problem

Close with readiness

Confident statement about your preparation to excel at MIT’s rigorous program

Have you built or used financial models in real work settings?

column2: The MAM is the second graduate degree following your MBA. What do you hope to get out of this degree to complement your MBA education? How will your MAM help you advance your impact not only in business but especially in society?
column7:
Crux of the Topic
Yale SOM wants to know:

Why you need the MAM after your MBA — what gaps it will fill, skills it will deepen, and perspectives it will broaden.

How you’ll leverage this added layer of knowledge and network to create meaningful impact in both business and society.

Your alignment with Yale SOM’s mission of “educating leaders for business and society.”

How to Approach It
Start with your MBA gains — leadership development, business fundamentals, strategic thinking.

Identify specific gaps you want to address — global exposure, interdisciplinary perspectives, deeper specialization, cross-cultural leadership.

Show how MAM’s unique offerings — flexible electives, Global Network for Advanced Management, access to Yale’s broader graduate schools, and SOM’s social impact focus — will bridge those gaps.

Highlight dual-impact vision — how you’ll apply your enhanced capabilities to drive innovation, profitability, and societal benefit.

End with forward-looking commitment as a Yale SOM alum.
column8:
What did your MBA give you that you now want to build upon?

Which skills or perspectives do you still want to develop before your next leadership role?

Which Yale MAM resources uniquely address these gaps?

How will your enhanced capabilities create societal as well as business impact?

What legacy do you want to leave as a Yale SOM alum?

column2: MAM graduates go on to pursue global careers in different fields and industries. What do you envision for your future career? What parts of the MAM program, both the flexible curriculum and resources Yale has to offer, will help you achieve this career goal?
column7:
Crux of the Topic
Yale SOM wants to see:

A clear, ambitious yet realistic career vision — ideally with global scope.

An understanding of how Yale’s MAM program specifically supports that vision — linking the flexible curriculum and unique resources to your needs.

Evidence of fit — that you’ll use Yale’s resources strategically and contribute back to the community.

How to Approach It
Lead with your future vision — define your target role, industry, and global impact.

Show why you’re aiming for this path — connect to past experience and personal motivation.

Highlight Yale MAM fit — mention relevant electives, Global Network for Advanced Management, cross-school course access, career resources, alumni network, and SOM’s mission of educating leaders for business and society.

Make the link tight and intentional — every Yale feature you mention should tie directly to a skill or network you need.

End with a forward-looking statement about impact and being a future Yale ambassador.
column8:
What is your ideal post-MAM role, and in which industry/region?

What global challenges or opportunities do you want to address?

Which Yale MAM courses or electives will directly help you in this career?

How will you leverage Yale’s networks and resources globally?

What impact do you hope to have as a Yale SOM graduate?

column2: We are looking for excellent candidates to join the MAM program. Excellence can take many different forms. Explain what excellence means to you and why you will be an excellent MAM student and future Yale SOM alum
column7:
Crux of the Topic
Yale SOM is looking for:

Your personal definition of excellence — beyond generic success; it should be values-driven and authentic.

Evidence you embody excellence — through leadership, collaboration, innovation, resilience, or social impact.

A forward connection — how your excellence will contribute to Yale’s community and your future as an alum.

How to Approach It
Start with your definition — grounded in values like integrity, impact, adaptability, or continuous learning.

Support with 1–2 short, high-impact examples — academic, professional, or personal achievements that demonstrate your standard of excellence.

Make the Yale link — show how you’ll bring these qualities to the MAM classroom, projects, and the global alumni network.

End forward-looking — your vision as a Yale alum creating positive change.

How do you personally define excellence in life and work?

What achievement best reflects your highest personal standard?

What will you bring to Yale’s MAM program that others might not?

How will you embody excellence as a Yale SOM alum?

column2:
Please upload a copy of your Supplemental Essay. The Graduate School is committed to fostering a welcoming and inclusive academic community and educating and training a distinguished and diverse cohort of students. Exposure to a broad range of perspectives, backgrounds, and experiences is essential to fostering both breadth and depth in intellectual knowledge and is an educational benefit.

At Brown, the term "diversity" is used in the broadest sense to encompass many dimensions of students' backgrounds, perspectives, and experiences. Brown welcomes applications to its graduate programs from all qualified individuals, regardless of race, color, religion, age, national or ethnic origin, caste, disability, status as a veteran, language, socio-economic background, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity, gender expression, political ideology, or theoretical approach.

In 300 words or less, please respond to at least one of the following prompts:

Discuss specific examples of how your background, knowledge, and life experiences would influence your ability to both benefit from and contribute to Brown’s inclusive academic community;

Discuss how you aspire to contribute to our mission to promote diversity and inclusion through your research, teaching, or service.
column7:
Got it — here are your confirmed details for Brown University, MFA in Literary Arts:

Essay 1: Supplemental Essay — Diversity & Inclusion focus — 300 words max

Essay 2: Personal Statement — No fixed word count (likely 1–2 pages)

Essay 1 — Supplemental Essay (Diversity & Inclusion)
Crux of the Topic
Brown is evaluating:

How your background, experiences, and worldview shape your engagement with diverse perspectives

Your readiness to thrive in and contribute to an inclusive academic community

Commitment to diversity and inclusion in practice — through writing, research, teaching, or service

How to Approach It
Choose your angle:

Either focus on how your background shapes your contributions

Or focus on how you actively promote diversity in your creative and academic work

You can also combine both

Be specific:

Include 1–2 real examples (from writing, teaching, activism, or community work)

Show impact — how you created space for underrepresented voices or learned from perspectives unlike your own

Tie to Brown:

Connect your approach to Brown’s mission and inclusive MFA community
column8:
What personal experience most shaped your view on diversity in the arts?

How has your work amplified marginalized or underrepresented voices?

When have you learned the most from a perspective unlike your own?

How do you integrate diversity into your creative process?

How will you promote inclusivity in Brown’s literary community?

column2:
Video Guidelines: Programme Guidance
High production qualities are not needed. We will review the work in your portfolio so keep your video simple. We are interested in what you say, to find out about you, your thinking, background and ambitions. Respond to a minimum of three of the following prompts. Consider these as the types of questions you would be asked in a face to face interview.

Who or what inspires you? 

How do you hope to incorporate this inspiration into your work?

What are your aspirations for your future professional life and how will IDE help you get there?

Talk about what unique skills or perspectives you will bring to the IDE student group.

General Information
​​​​​​​Your video does not need to be professionally shot, you may use the best resource available to you; we are not assessing the technical quality of this video
You must be visible in the video and communicate verbally yourself. 
Your video can be in any of these formats: avi, mp4, mov, wmv.
Your video must be a maximum of 2 minutes long, in real time.
column7:
Crux of the Topic
They want to quickly see:

Your design identity and creative influences

How inspiration translates into your process

A clear professional vision and why IDE is the right platform

A taste of your personality and communication style

How to Approach It — Step-by-Step
Choose 3 prompts that let you show different facets (e.g., inspiration, application, and contribution).

Keep your tone conversational but precise — 20–30 seconds per point.

Use micro-stories — instead of saying “I’m inspired by nature,” describe a single scene where this inspiration hit you.

Always connect back to how IDE will accelerate your work.
column8:

How does this influence show up in your work?

Show how design can reframe the problem or unlock solutions.

column2:
Personal History and Diversity Statement (Required for all Programs)
UC Santa Barbara is interested in a diverse and inclusive graduate student population. Please describe any aspects of your personal background, accomplishments, or achievements that you feel are important in evaluating your application for graduate study. For example, please describe if you have experienced economic challenges in achieving higher education, such as being financially responsible for family members or dependents, having to work significant hours during undergraduate schooling or coming from a family background of limited income. Please describe if you have any unusual or varied life experiences that might contribute to the diversity of the graduate group, such as fluency in other languages, experience living in bicultural communities, academic research interests focusing on cultural, societal, or educational problems as they affect underserved segments of society, or evidence of an intention to use the graduate degree toward serving disadvantaged individuals or populations.
column7:
Crux of the Topic
UCSB is assessing how your unique life experiences, background, and perspectives will enrich their graduate community and how your personal journey demonstrates resilience, commitment to diversity, and a capacity to contribute beyond academics. They want to see evidence of self-awareness, social awareness, and purposeful goals, especially in ways that connect to underrepresented or disadvantaged communities.

How to Approach It
Start with a defining personal background element — e.g., cultural heritage, socio-economic challenge, unusual upbringing, or bicultural identity — that has shaped who you are.

Highlight moments of resilience — concrete examples where you overcame obstacles, worked while studying, supported family, or navigated unfamiliar environments.

Show diversity contribution — share language skills, community involvement, or research/work addressing underserved populations.

Tie directly to computer science — explain how your unique perspective informs your research goals, choice of problems to solve, or long-term career plan to help disadvantaged or underrepresented groups.

Reflect, don’t just list — describe what these experiences taught you and how they will shape your role at UCSB and beyond.
column8:
What early life experience most shaped your worldview and personal values?

When have you had to overcome significant non-academic challenges to succeed?

How has your background influenced the way you approach problem-solving in computer science?

What communities or groups have you meaningfully impacted through your skills or perspective?

How will your UCSB education help you address inequities or expand access in tech?

column2:
Provide examples from your past professional or academic experience that highlight skills you have developed that will be directly applicable in your above-selected functional area. 

Chosen Functional Area after Brainstorming: Strats and Modelling
Chosen Industry after Brainstorming: Asset Management
column7:
Crux of the Topic
CMU is evaluating your technical and analytical readiness for a high-intensity quantitative finance program and your ability to apply these skills in Strats and Modelling for Asset Management. They want evidence from your past experiences — academic or professional — that shows you already have the core quantitative, programming, and problem-solving abilities needed for success.

How to Approach It
Select 2–3 experiences (mix of academic and professional) that best showcase skills like quantitative modeling, programming in Python/R/Matlab, data analysis, and financial modeling.

Make direct links between the skills demonstrated in those experiences and the skills required for Strats and Modelling roles.

Use concrete examples with measurable outcomes — e.g., accuracy improvement in a predictive model, efficiency gains, or successful portfolio optimization.

Keep asset management relevance clear — show that you understand how these skills are applied in real-world portfolio construction, risk management, or alpha generation.
column8:
Which past project best demonstrates your ability to build or refine financial models?

How have you applied programming to solve quantitative finance problems?

When did you first use data analysis to inform investment or risk decisions?

Which mathematical techniques have you applied most effectively in asset management contexts?

How will CMU help you advance your Strats and Modelling expertise?

column2: Essay Question (copy/pasted from the application form):	Tell us about a time you had to overcome a challenge in order to succeed.
column7:
Crux of the Topic
CMU wants to evaluate your resilience, problem-solving ability, and ability to perform under pressure — traits essential for success in the intense, quantitative MCF program. They’re looking for a specific, high-stakes situation where you encountered an obstacle, adapted, and ultimately succeeded.

How to Approach It
Choose one challenge (professional, academic, or personal) that is directly or indirectly related to the skills needed in computational finance — analytical thinking, time management, adaptability, or leadership under pressure.

Use a STAR format (Situation, Task, Action, Result) to keep it concise and impactful.

Show both technical and soft skills — not just what you did, but how you managed stress, motivated yourself/others, or adapted to changing conditions.

End with a reflection on lessons learned and how they prepared you for CMU’s program.
column8:
What was the most difficult obstacle you faced in a project or academic setting?

How did you adapt when initial strategies to solve a problem failed?

What skills were most critical in helping you overcome this challenge?

What was the measurable outcome of your effort?

How will this experience help you in CMU’s rigorous program?

column2: Optional Essay: Is there anything else that you think we should know as we evaluate your application? If you believe your credentials and essays represent you fairly, you should not feel obligated to answer this question. This essay is intended to provide a place for you to add information that you think is important but is not covered elsewhere in the application. This could include clarification of your employment or academic record, choice of recommenders or helpful context for the admissions committee in reviewing your application.
column7:
Crux of the Topic
This essay is a flexible space to:

Address potential concerns (e.g., academic gaps, low grades, employment changes, recommendation choices).

Provide context for unusual career moves or life circumstances.

Highlight important achievements or experiences not covered elsewhere in your application.

It’s not mandatory — you should only use it if it will strengthen your case or explain something an admissions officer might question.

How to Approach It
Clarification / Context (if relevant)

If you have academic dips, career gaps, unusual transcripts, or recommendation choices, explain them honestly and briefly.

Focus on lessons learned and how you’ve addressed the issue.

Value-Add Content (if relevant)

Share impactful experiences or achievements that didn’t fit elsewhere.

This could include research, publications, competitions, volunteer work, certifications, or unique personal projects — especially if they relate to computational finance skills.

Forward Connection

Link the additional information to how you will succeed in CMU’s MCF program.

Keep the tone positive and forward-looking, not defensive.
column8:
Is there any academic or employment gap that might need explanation?

Are there achievements or projects not covered in other essays that showcase MCF readiness?

Have you earned certifications, awards, or publications relevant to computational finance?

Have you faced challenges that shaped your resilience but didn’t fit the main essays?

How does this new information strengthen your fit for CMU?

column2: Describe the specific steps you took and resources you utilized to secure a previous academic or professional opportunity. Explain how this experience will benefit you in your future career path.

How to Approach It
Pick one clear example — ideally finance/quant-related, but any experience showing initiative and skill is fine.

Keep the sequence tight: situation → actions taken → resources used → skills gained → career link.

Use action verbs and measurable impact.

End with a forward connection to MCF and your asset management/quant goals.
column8:
Which past opportunity best showcases your initiative and persistence?

Who or what resources were most instrumental in your success?

What key skills did you gain from that experience?

How will these skills directly help you in your finance career?

Highlight technical growth — connect your personal journey to your computational science focus.

column2: Please attach a statement of two to three pages that will inform the Admissions Committee about the applicant's experience in legal practice and interest in graduate study (in the applicable specialization). This statement should also discuss the applicant's professional goals, and how the completion of an LLM degree could benefit his or her legal career.
column7:
Crux of the Topic
Stanford’s Admissions Committee wants to assess:

The depth and quality of your legal practice experience

A clear academic and professional focus in your chosen specialization

Motivation and readiness for advanced legal study at Stanford

A well-articulated career trajectory and how Stanford’s resources will help you achieve it

Fit with Stanford — they want applicants who will contribute to and benefit from their unique, collaborative, and interdisciplinary environment

This is both a professional narrative and a future-oriented plan. It should be deeply personal, authentic, and strategic. 

How to Approach It
Start with a compelling personal or professional moment

Open with a vivid anecdote or pivotal case/experience that shaped your legal perspective.

Choose something that illustrates both your technical skills and your values.

Showcase your professional journey

Provide context: area(s) of legal practice, jurisdictions, client types, and notable achievements.

Highlight leadership, problem-solving, and ethical decision-making.

Connect to your academic interest

Explain why you are drawn to your specific LLM specialization (e.g., corporate law, human rights, IP).

Use real examples from your work where you saw knowledge gaps or systemic challenges that advanced study could address.

Articulate professional goals

Be clear and concrete — short-term (immediately after LLM) and long-term (5–10 years).

Show awareness of the legal landscape and how your specialization positions you to make an impact.

Explain why Stanford

Reference faculty, research centers, clinics, seminars, or the interdisciplinary approach that align with your goals.

Demonstrate you’ve researched the program and can see yourself contributing to it.

Crux of the Topic
Rutgers wants to see whether you have:

How to Approach It

Share the personal, academic, or professional journey that sparked your interest in healthcare analytics.

Highlight a moment, challenge, or insight that made you realize the importance of data-driven solutions in healthcare.

Show Contribution

Demonstrate how your background, perspective, or skills will enhance class discussions, group projects, or networking within Rutgers.

Emphasize teamwork, cultural awareness, leadership, or unique expertise.

Project Future Impact

Articulate your career goals and how Rutgers’ program will help you achieve them.

Show awareness of healthcare challenges (costs, equity, technology adoption, patient outcomes) and explain how you want to make a difference.

Keep the tone authentic, forward-looking, and purpose-driven.

column2:
Personal Statement (Maximum: 1000 words) 
Needs to have details encompassing the following (as stated by the university): explain your motivation for applying for the course at Oxford, your relevant experience and education, and the specific areas that interest you and/or you intend to specialise in. If possible, please ensure that the word count is clearly displayed on the document.

Your statement should focus on scientific interests and motivation rather than personal achievements, interests and aspirations. This will be assessed for:

• your reasons for applying evidence of motivation for and understanding of the proposed area of study
• commitment to the subject beyond the requirements of the degree course
• preliminary knowledge of research techniques
• capacity for sustained and intense work
• reasoning ability
• ability to absorb new ideas, often presented abstractly, at a rapid pace.
column7:
Crux of the Topic
Oxford is testing whether you have:

Academic maturity: A deep, evidence-based interest in neuroscience rather than a superficial passion

Preparedness: Exposure to relevant education, research techniques, or projects that show you can thrive in a rigorous program

Focus: Clarity about which subfields in neuroscience excite you (e.g., computational neuroscience, neurodegenerative diseases, neural circuits, cognitive neuroscience)

Capacity for research: The ability to sustain intense academic work, reason critically, and handle abstract ideas

They are not looking for a “life story,” but for a scientific narrative that demonstrates your intellectual curiosity, research skills, and future goals.

How to Approach It
Begin with a Scientific Motivation

Instead of a personal anecdote, start with the research question, phenomenon, or neuroscience puzzle that first gripped your mind.

Show genuine curiosity about how the brain works and connect it to your desire to study at Oxford.

Showcase Relevant Academic & Research Experience

Highlight coursework, lab projects, research assistantships, internships, or thesis work that gave you technical knowledge.

Emphasize your hands-on experience with neuroscience techniques (e.g., fMRI, EEG, microscopy, computational modeling, genetics, behavioral studies).

Demonstrate not just participation, but insight gained from these experiences.

Define Your Specific Research Interests

Be concrete: Which subfields at Oxford fascinate you most?

Mention faculty, labs, or research themes you’d like to engage with (if applicable).

Show preliminary understanding of the methods used in those areas.

Demonstrate Your Academic Traits

Illustrate moments where you handled complex, abstract, or interdisciplinary ideas.

Give evidence of your resilience, curiosity, and commitment to neuroscience beyond class requirements (e.g., extra readings, seminars, independent projects).

Conclude with Future Goals

Briefly outline how this MSc fits into your long-term path (PhD, clinical research, biotech, academia, etc.).

Make the connection between Oxford’s resources and your ability to answer meaningful neuroscience questions.
column8:
What neuroscience phenomenon or problem first captured your intellectual curiosity?

Which research techniques have you practiced, and what did you learn from applying them?

Can you describe a challenging neuroscience project and how you overcame obstacles?

Which specific subfields or faculty at Oxford align with your research interests?

How will this MSc program prepare you for your long-term neuroscience goals?

column2:
Your Statement of Purpose should describe your academic and professional background, your objectives for graduate school, and how you will positively contribute to the Teachers College community.

Your statement should be written by you alone and represent your original work. Please note that the Admission Committee will carefully consider both the content and quality of the writing in their assessment of your candidacy for graduate study.

At Teachers College, our mission is to empower committed learners and leaders to build a smarter, healthier, more just and equitable world. We strive to establish an institution of diverse students from all backgrounds dedicated to multidisciplinary knowledge creation, policy engagement, and practice innovations across education, psychology, and health.
•	Please discuss your objectives for pursuing graduate study at Teachers College, and how the program in Neuroscience and Education will enable you to achieve these goals. Be sure to highlight your academic and professional background and how your experiences have led you to apply to Teachers College.
•	At Teachers College, we value our students’ personal strengths, talents, and the lived experiences that have shaped them. Please share with us how you believe your specific strengths, talents, and experiences have developed and influenced you, and how these will positively contribute to a diverse Teachers College community and the world beyond.
column7:
Crux of the Topic
Teachers College is looking for evidence that you are not only academically prepared for an interdisciplinary program but also deeply aligned with its mission of equity, health, and education through neuroscience. They want to see:

Academic & professional readiness: Past studies, research, and work experiences that show you’re prepared for graduate-level neuroscience and education.

Clear objectives: A focused vision of what you hope to accomplish through this program and beyond.

Contribution to community: Your personal strengths, talents, and lived experiences that will enrich a diverse and collaborative TC community.

Commitment to impact: Alignment with TC’s mission of building a healthier, more just, and equitable world.

How to Approach It
Introduction: Set Your Purpose

Open with the central motivation driving you to pursue neuroscience and education (e.g., a compelling academic or professional observation).

Establish how your background uniquely positions you for this program.

Show Academic & Professional Background

Detail your relevant coursework, research, and work experience bridging neuroscience, psychology, education, or health.

Highlight the skills you’ve gained (data analysis, research techniques, educational interventions, teaching, community outreach).

Define Your Graduate Objectives at Teachers College

Explain why TC’s Neuroscience and Education program is the right fit.

Reference the program’s multidisciplinary nature — education, psychology, and health — and how this matches your career vision.

If relevant, mention specific research areas, faculty, or labs at TC that resonate with your goals.

Show How You’ll Contribute to the Community

Highlight personal strengths, talents, or lived experiences that shaped your perspective.

Emphasize leadership, collaboration, or advocacy roles that demonstrate your ability to enrich TC’s diverse student body.

Connect to the Bigger Mission

Show how your work will extend beyond TC to advance equity, justice, and innovation in education and neuroscience.

Position yourself as a future leader committed to impactful change.
column8:
What moment or experience convinced you to bridge neuroscience with education?

Which academic or professional projects best prepared you for this interdisciplinary field?

How does Teachers College’s mission align with your personal and career vision?

What unique strengths, talents, or experiences will you bring to enrich the TC community?

In what ways do you hope to create impact in education and neuroscience after graduation?

Educational Background & Professional Experience

Crux of the Topic
Brown + RISD want to see:

Motivation for combining design + engineering

A track record of multidisciplinary skill — showing you can bridge technical rigor and creative problem-solving

Clear understanding of issues you want to tackle — ideally complex, societal, or system-level problems

Why this joint program is uniquely suited to your goals

A sense of your professional vision and impact

How to Approach It — Step-by-Step
Hook with a personal turning point — the moment you realised design and engineering must co-exist in your work (could be a project failure, breakthrough, or real-world observation).

Demonstrate academic preparation — core design principles, engineering fundamentals, and how you’ve already merged them.

Highlight signature projects — 2–3 examples showing you can integrate creativity with problem-solving. For each:

The problem you tackled

Your role and responsibilities

Methodology and tools used

Outcomes (impact, innovation, adoption)

Identify pressing issues you want to address (sustainability, human-centred tech, accessibility) — why these matter now.

Explain why Brown + RISD is essential — specific faculty, labs, cross-campus collaboration, methodology.

Describe your professional trajectory — where you see yourself 5–10 years after the program.

column7:
Crux of the Topic
MIT is assessing:

Motivation: Why supply chain management and why now?

Preparation: How your academic, professional, and personal experiences have prepared you.

Fit: Why MIT’s SCM program specifically — including unique aspects like research focus, faculty, curriculum, and industry ties.

Future Impact: Your short- and long-term vision, and how MIT will bridge the gap between your current capabilities and future goals.

They want a candidate who is self-aware, has clear direction, and can demonstrate tangible impact in the supply chain field after graduating.

How to Approach It
Anchor in a formative experience: Start with a concrete story or moment that sparked your interest in supply chain.

Highlight progressive preparation: Show how your academic studies, internships, and professional roles deepened your expertise. Include personal influences if relevant (e.g., global exposure, cultural experiences, entrepreneurial challenges).

Link to MIT SCM specifically: Mention the program’s analytical rigor, MITx courses, research labs (e.g., MIT Center for Transportation & Logistics), industry projects, or faculty mentors you admire.

Bridge to future goals: Lay out your professional vision in a clear sequence — what you’ll do immediately after graduation, and how it positions you for long-term leadership in supply chain innovation.

Weave in personal qualities: Show evidence of leadership, problem-solving, resilience, and collaboration — the “human” side of your candidacy.
column8:
What single event first made you curious about supply chain management?

Which academic projects best prepared you for MIT’s SCM rigor?

What’s the most complex supply chain challenge you’ve solved professionally?

How does MIT’s program uniquely fit your learning style and career plan?

In 10 years, what impact do you want to have on global supply chains?

column2:

Conciseness: You have ~200–220 words, so every sentence must carry weight.

How to Approach It
Lead with a compelling hook: One sentence summarizing your background and what drew you to technology management.

Showcase preparation: Highlight two to three concrete experiences — an academic project, professional achievement, and/or leadership role.

Connect to NYU Tandon: Name specific aspects like innovation labs, industry connections, or faculty expertise that align with your interests.

End with your 5-year plan: Clearly state the role you envision, the industry you’ll impact, and how you’ll measure success.

column5: Masters of Science in Engineering Management
column7:
Crux of the Topic
USC’s admissions committee wants to see a clear, compelling narrative that connects:

Your academic foundation in engineering.

Your leadership and management potential.

Your motivation and fit for USC’s Engineering Management program.

Your career vision and anticipated contributions to the field.

They want proof you are both technically capable and strategically minded — someone who can lead in complex engineering environments.

Select 2–3 anchor experiences that best illustrate your evolution from a technical contributor to a potential engineering leader.

Integrate quantifiable impact — results, efficiencies, cost savings, innovations.

Make USC-specific connections — show you’ve researched their curriculum, professors, labs, or student organizations.

End with a bold but realistic vision of the difference you’ll make in your career.

column2:
"The Personal Statement is an opportunity for you to provide additional information that may aid the selection committee in evaluating your preparation and aptitude for graduate study at UCLA. It will also be used to consider candidates for the Cota-Robles and Graduate Opportunity fellowships.

Instructions

Please respond to one or more of the following prompts. Your statement can be up to 500 words in length (approximately 1-page, single spaced, using 1-inch margins and 12-point font). To be considered for a Cota-Robles or Graduate Opportunity fellowship, be sure to describe your contributions to diversity. The University of California Diversity Statement can be found online

    Are there educational, personal, cultural, economic, or social experiences, not described in your Statement of Purpose, that have shaped your academic journey? If so, how? Have any of these experiences provided unique perspective(s) that you would contribute to your program, field or profession?
     
    Describe challenge(s) or barriers that you have faced in your pursuit of higher education. What motivated you to persist, and how did you overcome them? What is the evidence of your persistence, progress or success?
     
    How have your life experiences and educational background informed your understanding of the barriers facing groups that are underrepresented in higher education?
     
    How have you been actively engaged (e.g., through participation, employment, service, teaching or other activities) in programs or activities focused on increasing participation by groups that have been historically underrepresented in higher education?
     
    How do you intend to engage in scholarly discourse, research, teaching, creative efforts, and/or community engagement during your graduate program that have the potential to advance diversity and equal opportunity in higher education?
     
    How do you see yourself contributing to diversity in your profession after you earn your advanced degree at UCLA?
"

Your life experiences (educational, personal, cultural, social, economic) that shaped your academic path.

Potential fit for the Cota-Robles and Graduate Opportunity Fellowships — which prioritize leadership, resilience, and service toward diversity goals.

They’re not asking for research goals here — that’s for your SOP. This is about personal journey + contributions to diversity in academic and professional settings.

column2:
"What is the statement of purpose, and what is it used for?

The statement of purpose is an integral part of your application for graduate admission and consideration for merit-based financial support. It is used to understand your academic interests, and to evaluate your aptitude and preparation for graduate work, as well as your fit with the proposed program of study. It is also used to assess your ability to write coherent and convincing prose.

Instructions

Please respond to the following. Your statement can be up to 500 words in length (approximately 1-page, single spaced, using 1-inch margins and 12-point font). You do not need to answer every question; focus on the elements that you feel are most relevant to your candidacy.

What is your purpose in applying for graduate study in your specified degree program? Describe your area(s) of interest, including any subfield(s) or interdisciplinary interests.
 
What experiences have prepared you for advanced study or research in this degree program? What relevant skills have you gained from these experiences? Have your experiences led to specific or tangible outcomes that would support your potential to contribute to this field (examples: performances, publications, presentations, awards or recognitions)?
 
What additional information about your past experience may aid the selection committee in evaluating your preparation and aptitude for graduate study at UCLA? For example, you may wish to describe research, employment, teaching, service, artistic or international experiences through which you have developed skills in leadership, communication, project management, teamwork, or other areas.
 
Why is the UCLA graduate program to which you are applying is the best place for you to pursue your academic goals? If you are applying for a research master’s or doctoral program, we encourage you to indicate specific research interests and potential faculty mentors.
 
What are your plans for your career after earning this degree?"
column7:
Crux of the Topic
UCLA is looking to assess:

Your purpose for pursuing a Master’s in Business Analytics now.

Specific academic and professional experiences that prepared you for this program.

Relevant skills (quantitative, technical, leadership, communication) and tangible outcomes.

Fit with UCLA — knowledge of the program’s structure, faculty, or unique opportunities.

Your career trajectory — short- and long-term goals, and how UCLA bridges them.
column8:
1. What was your most impactful analytics project, and what did you learn?

4. Which UCLA resource or faculty member excites you most, and why?
5. What is your long-term vision for using analytics in your career?

column2:
"As a reminder, competitive candidates need not have prior direct experience in an education setting though should have a clear conception of why they’re pursuing a master’s degree in education.  In addition, “education” and “education-related” are broadly defined at HGSE and encompass all fields, roles, and contexts covered by the Ed.M. Programs and Concentrations we offer.

In your statement, please discuss:

Your background: what key experiences have contributed to your commitment to work in the field of education or with education-related issues
Your objectives for graduate study: what are your academic and professional goals and what knowledge, skills, and tools are you hoping to develop through enrollment in a graduate program
Why HGSE, and why this specific Program (and Concentration, if relevant): why is enrollment at HGSE, and in this master’s Program and Concentration specifically, critical in helping you achieve your goals"
column7:

HGSE wants to evaluate:

Your personal and professional background — experiences that shaped your interest in education or education-related work.

Your objectives for graduate study — both academic and career goals.

Your understanding of education as HGSE defines it — broad, interdisciplinary, and spanning multiple fields.

Your fit with HGSE — why this specific program and (if applicable) concentration aligns with your goals.

Your potential to contribute meaningfully to the HGSE community and your field after graduation
column8:

3. Which skills or knowledge areas do you most want to develop at HGSE?

column2:
"Essays (Required)
As part of your application, you are expected to provide a response, in written form, to two prompts. A response to both prompts is required. This is an opportunity for you to give us a stronger sense of who you are, as a person. Your essay should be as unique as you and should be written using your own voice. Include each prompt as the header for each of your two responses.
When finished, combine your response to both prompts into one, single PDF and upload it below. Your total essay upload should be 12pt Arial font and no more than two pages, double-spaced, in length.

Prompt 1: Drawing from your previous academic, professional and personal experiences, tell us what has attracted you to the program of study you have noted in your application. Be very specific about your past experiences and how they shaped your interests. 

Prompt 2: Students often have a wide range of ideas and plans for what they hope to achieve after they receive their graduate degree. Tell us what your 5-year plan is after completing a degree from NYU Tandon."
column7:

Prompt 1: How your academic, professional, and personal history led you to cybersecurity.

Prompt 2: A clear, realistic, and ambitious 5-year post-graduation plan.

Your ability to communicate a cohesive, personal narrative in two linked yet distinct sections.

5. What industry problems do you want to be solving 5 years from now?

column2:
Personal Statement: 

You will upload your Personal Statement to the MyStatus portal. Limit your statement to 750 words.

Think of this statement as a lens through which other elements of your application can be brought into focus for us. What you include is your call, though it’s not uncommon for writers to discuss the work (books, films, plays, etc.) that has shaped their writing lives. If you’ve had jobs or travels that seem relevant to who you are as a writer, we’d be glad to hear about them. If you have some special preparation or background in the secondary field, it’s worth briefly mentioning in the statement.

Include a header with your name, your declared primary and secondary genres, and “Michener Center for Writers.”

Connect your journey to your readiness for the Michener MFA

column2:
Supplementary Personal Statement

Needs to have details encompassing the following (as stated by the university): The supporting statement is your opportunity to address the academic and any non-academic entry requirements as described in the prospectus. You should address the following:
Your academic interests and reasons for applying to this programme.
Your career objectives and any relevant non-academic achievements as well as any publications. Outline any other relevant experience including attendance at specialist workshops or short courses.
Applicants for taught programmes, in particular flexible programmes, should indicate, where appropriate, the options/modules in which they are likely to be interested.
Should you wish accreditation of prior learning to be considered as part of your application please detail the reason for your request and course units from which exemption is sought, including courses taken and the equivalent UCL courses, e.g. "Biology B100, Cellular and Molecular Biology, 1 course unit”.
column7:
Crux of the Topic
UCL is assessing whether you have the academic readiness, relevant experience, and clear career goals to thrive in their MSc Neuroscience program. They want to see:

Depth of academic interest in neuroscience and alignment with UCL’s modules

Evidence of preparation through prior coursework, research, and specialized learning opportunities

Professional vision for how this MSc supports your long-term career

Holistic fit by considering relevant non-academic achievements, workshops, or publications

Awareness of UCL’s structure (modules/options) and possibly accreditation requests (if applicable)

How to Approach It
Start with Academic Interests

Frame your statement around the neuroscience questions, problems, or themes that most excite you.

Show you’re applying because UCL’s program specifically matches these interests.

Demonstrate Academic Preparation & Experience

Highlight coursework, research projects, lab skills, or relevant techniques (e.g., neuroimaging, computational methods, molecular biology).

Mention specialist workshops, seminars, or certifications if applicable.

Note any publications or posters, even local or undergraduate-level.

Highlight Career Objectives & Broader Achievements

Be concrete about your post-MSc vision (PhD, clinical neuroscience, biotech, neuroinformatics, etc.).

Tie in relevant leadership roles, community engagement, or non-academic achievements that show your broader strengths.

Show Fit with UCL’s Program

If possible, name the modules/options that align with your goals.

Explain why UCL’s approach (interdisciplinary, flexible, research-driven) is ideal for you.

Address Accreditation (if relevant)

If you’re requesting exemption for prior learning, detail clearly the course units already taken and their UCL equivalents.

If not, you can skip this.

Share a concise background (e.g., cultural, socioeconomic, first-generation, or underrepresented identity) that shaped your journey.

column2:
Please read parts (a) and (b) below carefully and write an essay addressing both questions, with part (a) constituting at least half of the total length. Footnotes do not count towards the overall word limit as long as they are limited to providing sources and citations. Your entire statement should be no more than 1,500 words—anything exceeding the word limit will be disallowed. Please type your statement with your full name on the top of each page.
a.	Briefly describe either an important issue in your field of interest or a current legal problem facing a particular country, region, or the world, and then propose and analyse a theoretical framework or legal strategy to address this issue. For the avoidance of doubt, your response should not be limited to outlining a research plan or proposal. Successful essays will contain substantive analysis of the issue identified. 

b.	Please tell us something about yourself—in particular, why you wish to pursue an LL.M. degree at Harvard and how doing so connects with what you have done in the past and what you plan to do in the future.
column7:
Crux of the Topic
Harvard’s Admissions Committee is assessing:

Depth of legal knowledge and ability to analyse complex issues (Part a)

Critical thinking and originality in proposing a theoretical or legal strategy

Clarity of personal motivation for pursuing an LLM at Harvard (Part b)

Professional maturity and fit — they want to see that your goals, values, and prior work align with Harvard’s mission and resources

Balance between intellectual rigor and personal narrative

This is an academic-analytical + personal-professional hybrid essay, requiring you to demonstrate both legal scholarship and compelling storytelling.

How to Approach It
Part (a) — At least 50% of total length (~800–900 words)
Pick an issue with depth and relevance

It should be significant in your field and globally/regionally relevant.

Examples: climate litigation, cross-border data privacy, AI regulation, constitutional reform, refugee rights, corporate accountability in supply chains, etc.

Frame the issue clearly

Start with a brief context-setting: scale, urgency, stakeholders affected.

Cite key cases, statutes, treaties, or legal principles as needed (footnotes allowed).

Analyse root causes & challenges

Show you understand not just the “what” but the “why” and “how” of the problem.

Propose a theoretical framework or legal strategy

Must be grounded in legal theory yet practical enough to be implementable.

Consider comparative law perspectives or interdisciplinary inputs.

Show originality — not just summarising existing solutions.

Critically analyse your own proposal

Anticipate challenges, limitations, and counterarguments.

Suggest ways to address these weaknesses.

Part (b) — Remaining ~600–700 words
Personal background and motivation

Connect your professional experiences and values to the legal issue discussed in Part (a).

Show how your past work has prepared you to tackle this kind of challenge.

Why Harvard LLM

Specific courses, faculty, clinics, research centres, or journals.

Harvard’s global network and interdisciplinary opportunities.

Future trajectory

Clear short-term and long-term goals.

Show how the Harvard LLM is a logical and necessary step in achieving them
column8:
What pressing legal issue in your field demands urgent, innovative solutions?

Which gaps in current legal responses frustrate you the most?

What original framework or legal tool could fill these gaps?

Which Harvard faculty, clinics, or courses directly support your proposed work?

How will your LLM-fueled expertise create measurable change in your country or field?
